lessons.title: Principles of Leveling
learning_objectives: Define leveling and explain how a level establishes a horizontal line of sight referenced to a level (equipotential) surface.
Distinguish among a level surface, a level line, a horizontal line, the vertical, and elevation.
State and apply the Height-of-Instrument (HI) method, including HI = BM elevation + BS and elevation = HI - FS.
Identify the major instruments and accessories used in differential leveling and what each contributes.
key_takeaways: Leveling determines differences in elevation by referencing a horizontal line of sight to a level surface.
A level surface is curved (follows gravity); a horizontal line is the straight tangent to it at the instrument.
The Height-of-Instrument method drives all differential leveling: HI = known elevation + backsight; new elevation = HI - foresight.
A backsight (BS or +) is read on a point of known elevation; a foresight (FS or -) is read on a point whose elevation is being determined.

content.html:
What Leveling Is

Leveling is the process of determining differences in elevation between points on, above, or below the surface of the earth. Where distance and angle measurement fix a point's horizontal position, leveling fixes its vertical position — its elevation, the vertical distance above or below a chosen reference surface (datum). Almost every surveying and engineering project depends on reliable elevations: setting grades for roads and pipelines, mapping topography, designing drainage, computing earthwork volumes, and establishing flood elevations all begin with a level circuit.

The instrument we call a level does one thing extraordinarily well: it provides a horizontal line of sight. When the line of sight is truly horizontal, the difference between two rod readings taken from that single setup equals the difference in elevation between the two points the rod was held on. From that single, simple idea — a horizontal line of sight and a graduated rod — the entire discipline of leveling is built.

Level Surfaces, Level Lines, and the Vertical

To level correctly you must keep five related terms straight:

  • A level surface is a continuous surface that is everywhere perpendicular to the direction of gravity (the plumb line). It is an equipotential surface — still water at rest lies on one. Because it follows gravity around the planet, a level surface is curved, roughly following the earth's shape.
  • A level line lies entirely within a level surface; it too is curved.
  • A horizontal line is a straight line tangent to the level surface at one point — the line of sight your level provides. Over short sights the horizontal line and the level line are nearly identical; over long sights they diverge (the basis of the curvature correction studied later).
  • The vertical is the direction of gravity (plumb) at a point, perpendicular to the level surface.
  • Elevation is the vertical distance from the datum (a chosen level surface) up or down to the point.

content.text: A horizontal line and a level line agree only at the instrument. Because the level line curves away (down) from the straight horizontal sight, a perfectly horizontal line of sight reads slightly HIGH on a distant rod. This is the source of the curvature-and-refraction correction covered in Lesson 4 and the datums lesson.

content.html:
The Height-of-Instrument (HI) Method

The workhorse computation of differential leveling is the Height-of-Instrument (HI) method. The HI is the elevation of the instrument's horizontal line of sight — not the height of the telescope above the ground, but the elevation of the line of sight above the datum.

The procedure has two repeating steps. First, set the level and take a backsight (BS): a rod reading on a point whose elevation is already known (a benchmark or turning point). Adding that reading to the known elevation gives the HI, because the rod reading is how far the line of sight rises above that known point. Second, take a foresight (FS): a rod reading on a new point whose elevation you want. Subtracting the foresight from the HI gives the new elevation, because the foresight is how far the line of sight rises above the new point.

Backsights are also called plus sights (+) because they are added to an elevation to get the HI; foresights are minus sights (-) because they are subtracted from the HI to get an elevation. Master these two equations and you can level anything.

content.latex: HI = Elev_{BM} + BS \qquad Elev_{pt} = HI - FS
content.label: Height-of-Instrument equations

rows: Rod reading on a point of KNOWN elevation; added to elevation to get HI
Elevation of the level's horizontal line of sight
Rod reading on a point of UNKNOWN elevation; subtracted from HI to get elevation
Permanent point of known, fixed elevation
Temporary point that receives an FS then a BS to advance the circuit

content.html:
Instruments and Accessories

A modern leveling outfit consists of a level (an automatic/self-leveling level with a compensator, a digital/electronic level reading a bar-coded rod, or a precise tilting level for high-order work), a sturdy tripod, and a graduated leveling rod (commonly graduated in feet and hundredths, or in meters). Accessories include a rod level (a bull's-eye bubble that keeps the rod plumb), a turning-point pin or plate (a stable, well-defined point for TPs), and a field book or data collector for recording the BS/HI/FS sequence. The single most common avoidable error in leveling is a rod that is not held plumb — a leaning rod always reads too long.

content.alt: Diagram of a level set between a benchmark and a turning point, showing a horizontal line of sight, a backsight rod reading on the benchmark, and a foresight rod reading on the turning point, with HI labeled.
content.caption: Figure: The HI method - BS added to a known elevation gives the line-of-sight elevation (HI); FS subtracted from HI gives the new point's elevation.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Leveling determines differences in elevation using a horizontal line of sight referenced to a level surface.
A level surface is curved (perpendicular to gravity); the line of sight is a straight horizontal tangent to it.
HI = known elevation + backsight; new elevation = HI - foresight.
Backsights (+) are read on known points; foresights (-) are read on points whose elevation is sought.
Keeping the rod plumb and the line of sight truly horizontal are the foundations of accurate leveling.

flashcards.definition: A continuous surface that is everywhere perpendicular to the direction of gravity; an equipotential surface that is curved, following the shape of the earth.
flashcards.hint_1: Still water at rest lies on one.

flashcards.definition: The elevation of the level's horizontal line of sight above the datum, computed as HI = known elevation + backsight.
flashcards.hint_1: Not the height of the telescope above the ground.

flashcards.definition: A rod reading taken on a point of known elevation; it is added to that elevation to obtain the HI.
flashcards.hint_1: Always read FIRST from a setup, on a known point.

flashcards.definition: A rod reading taken on a point of unknown elevation; it is subtracted from the HI to obtain that point's elevation.

lessons.title: Differential Leveling Procedures
learning_objectives: Carry an elevation from a benchmark to a distant point through a series of instrument setups and turning points (the level circuit).
Record and reduce level notes using the HI method, computing HI and elevations at each setup.
Use turning points correctly and explain why balanced backsight and foresight distances eliminate systematic errors.
Apply the arithmetic check (sum BS - sum FS = last elevation - first elevation) to a set of level notes.
key_takeaways: Differential leveling carries an elevation from a benchmark to other points through a chain of setups linked by turning points.
At each setup: HI = previous elevation + BS, then elevation of the next point = HI - FS.
Balancing backsight and foresight distances cancels curvature, refraction, and most collimation error.
Arithmetic check: (sum of backsights) - (sum of foresights) = (final elevation) - (starting elevation).

content.html:
Carrying an Elevation: The Level Circuit

Differential leveling determines the difference in elevation between two points by direct observation. When the two points are too far apart, too far apart in elevation, or obstructed so they cannot be seen from a single setup, the elevation is carried through a series of instrument setups connected by turning points. The whole chain — from the starting benchmark, through every setup and turning point, to the final point (and, for control work, back to a benchmark) — is the level circuit or level run.

At each setup the level is placed roughly midway between the previous point and the next. The surveyor reads a backsight on the previous point (known elevation), computes the HI, then reads a foresight on the next point and computes its elevation. That next point becomes a turning point (TP): a stable, well-defined point that receives a foresight from one setup and then a backsight from the next, passing the elevation forward like a baton.

Recording and Reducing Level Notes

Level notes are kept in columns: Station / BS (+) / HI / FS (-) / Elevation. The reduction follows the HI method mechanically. Begin at the benchmark with its known elevation. Add the backsight to get the first HI. Subtract the foresight on the first turning point to get that TP's elevation. Move the instrument forward, backsight the same TP to get a new HI, foresight the next TP, and so on. The starting benchmark's elevation is given; every other elevation in the table is computed.

Discipline in note-keeping is essential: read and record the backsight before disturbing the setup, hold the rod plumb on a firm point, and never assume — always carry the actual recorded numbers through the arithmetic. A single transposed digit propagates through every elevation that follows it.

content.text: Arithmetic check for the table above: Sum BS - Sum FS = 12.94 - 12.39 = +0.55 ft, and final - starting elevation = 100.55 - 100.00 = +0.55 ft. They agree, so the note reduction is arithmetically correct (this checks the math, NOT field accuracy).

content.html:
Why Balance Backsight and Foresight Distances

The most important field technique in differential leveling is keeping the backsight and foresight distances equal at each setup. Three systematic errors all depend on sight length: earth curvature (the level line curves away from the horizontal sight), atmospheric refraction (the line of sight bends), and collimation error (the line of sight is not perfectly horizontal because of imperfect instrument adjustment). Each of these makes a rod read incorrectly by an amount proportional to the sight distance.

When the backsight and foresight distances are equal, the same error appears in both readings and cancels in the difference, because each setup computes a difference (BS - FS). This is why a surveyor paces or otherwise balances sight lengths and avoids excessively long sights. Balanced sights are far more powerful than precise instrument adjustment for controlling these systematic errors.

sections.title: Open vs. closed level circuits
sections.content: An open level run ends on a different point than it started; it has no independent check on field accuracy. A closed level circuit either loops back to its starting benchmark or ties to a second benchmark of known elevation, providing a misclosure that measures the quality of the work (covered in Lesson 6). Control leveling should always be closed.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Differential leveling carries an elevation through setups linked by turning points (the level circuit).
Reduce notes with the HI method: HI = elevation + BS; next elevation = HI - FS.
Balance backsight and foresight distances to cancel curvature, refraction, and collimation error.
Apply the arithmetic check: sum BS - sum FS must equal final elevation - starting elevation.

flashcards.definition: A stable, well-defined temporary point that receives a foresight from one instrument setup and a backsight from the next, passing the elevation forward through a level circuit.
flashcards.hint_1: It gets both an FS and then a BS.

flashcards.definition: Keeping the backsight and foresight distances equal at each setup so that curvature, refraction, and collimation errors are the same in both readings and cancel in the BS - FS difference.
The complete chain of instrument setups and turning points used to carry an elevation from a starting benchmark to other points and, ideally, back to a benchmark.
A check that the note reduction is correct: the sum of backsights minus the sum of foresights must equal the final elevation minus the starting elevation.
flashcards.hint_1: It checks the math, not the field accuracy.

lessons.title: Profile and Cross-Section Leveling
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of profile leveling and how intermediate foresights produce a continuous ground profile along a line.
Distinguish intermediate sights (used for ground shots) from turning-point foresights (used to advance the circuit).
Describe cross-section leveling and how it supplies the data needed to compute earthwork volumes.
Read and interpret stationing, a profile plot, and a typical cross-section.
key_takeaways: Profile leveling records ground elevations at regular stations along a route to plot a vertical profile.
Intermediate foresights (IFS) take ground-elevation shots but do not advance the circuit; only turning points do.
Cross-section leveling measures ground elevations perpendicular to the centerline at each station for earthwork volumes.
Stationing (e.g., 12+50 = 1250 ft) locates each shot along the route.

content.html:
Profile Leveling

Profile leveling determines the elevation of the ground surface along a defined line — the centerline of a proposed road, pipeline, ditch, or channel. The result is a profile: a side-view plot of ground elevation against horizontal distance (stationing) that engineers use to set grades, design vertical curves, and balance cut and fill. Unlike pure differential leveling, whose only goal is to carry an accurate elevation from point to point, profile leveling collects many ground elevations along the way.

It does this with two kinds of foresight. Turning-point foresights advance the circuit and carry the elevation, exactly as in differential leveling, and must be taken on firm, recoverable points. Intermediate foresights (IFS) — also called ground shots or rod shots — are taken on the ground at each station (and at every break in slope) to record the profile. Intermediate sights do not change the HI and are not part of the elevation-carrying arithmetic; they are simply HI minus the rod reading at each station.

content.label: Ground elevation from an intermediate foresight

content.text: Read intermediate foresights only to the precision the job needs (often the nearest 0.1 ft for a ground profile), but read turning-point foresights and backsights to full precision (0.01 ft). The accuracy of the whole profile depends on the turning points, not the ground shots.

content.html:
Stationing

Positions along a route are described by stationing. A full station is 100 ft, written with a plus sign separating hundreds of feet from the remainder: station 0+00 is the beginning, 1+00 is 100 ft along, and 12+50 is 1,250 ft along. (In metric practice a full station is commonly 1,000 m or 100 m.) Profile shots are taken at each full or half station and at every significant grade break — the top and bottom of a bank, the edge of a creek, a change in slope — so the plotted profile faithfully represents the real ground.

Cross-Section Leveling

Cross-section leveling measures ground elevations along lines drawn perpendicular to the route centerline at each station, extending left and right far enough to cover the construction limits. Each cross-section is a transverse profile of the ground. When the existing-ground cross-sections are compared with the proposed design template (the finished roadway or channel shape), the difference in area at each station gives the cut or fill area; multiplying these end areas by the distance between stations and summing yields earthwork volumes (the average-end-area method, treated in the volumes module).

Cross-section shots are recorded as offsets from centerline (e.g., 25 ft Left, centerline, 25 ft Right) with the elevation at each offset. Together, profile and cross-section data form the complete existing-ground model an engineer needs to design a route and estimate quantities.

content.alt: A route profile plot of ground elevation versus stationing with a proposed grade line, alongside a typical cross-section showing offsets left and right of centerline with cut and fill areas.
content.caption: Figure: A profile (elevation vs. station) and a typical cross-section (elevation vs. offset) used together to design grade and compute earthwork.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Profile leveling records ground elevations along a centerline to plot a vertical profile for design.
Intermediate foresights take ground shots (Elev = HI - IFS) but do not advance the circuit; only turning points do.
Stationing (e.g., 12+50 = 1,250 ft) locates each shot along the route.
Cross-section leveling provides perpendicular ground profiles used with the average-end-area method to compute earthwork volumes.

flashcards.definition: Leveling that determines ground elevations at regular stations along a defined line (centerline) to produce a vertical profile used for grade design.
flashcards.hint_1: Side-view of the ground along a route.

flashcards.definition: A ground-elevation rod shot taken to record the profile; it equals HI minus the rod reading and does NOT advance the level circuit or change the HI.
A method of locating points along a route where a full station is 100 ft; e.g., station 12+50 is 1,250 ft from the beginning.
Leveling along lines perpendicular to the centerline at each station to capture the transverse ground profile, supplying end-area data for earthwork volume computation.

lessons.title: Trigonometric Leveling
learning_objectives: Explain how trigonometric leveling obtains a difference in elevation from a measured slope distance (or horizontal distance) and a vertical/zenith angle.
Apply the elevation-difference equations using either a vertical angle (dist x tan) or a zenith angle (S x cos zenith).
Compute a point's elevation accounting for instrument height (HI) and reflector/target height.
Recognize when curvature-and-refraction corrections and reciprocal observations are needed.
key_takeaways: Trigonometric leveling derives elevation difference from a distance and a vertical (or zenith) angle, not a level line of sight.
With a vertical angle a above horizontal: delta-elev = (horizontal distance) x tan(a); with a slope distance S: delta-elev = S x sin(a).
With a zenith angle z (from straight up): delta-elev = S x cos(z).
Full elevation = instrument elevation + instrument height + delta-elev - target height (plus curvature/refraction over long sights).

content.html:
Leveling with a Total Station

Trigonometric leveling determines a difference in elevation indirectly, from a measured distance and a measured vertical angle, rather than from a horizontal line of sight. It is the natural way a total station measures elevations: it observes the slope distance to a prism and the angle of the line of sight, and computes the vertical component trigonometrically. Trig leveling is efficient over rough or steep terrain where running a level circuit would require many setups, and it is the basis of how total-station topographic shots return an elevation along with a position.

The geometry is a right triangle. The measured line to the target is the hypotenuse (the slope distance S) or, with the horizontal distance known, one leg. The vertical angle of the line of sight fixes how that line rises or falls. The vertical leg of the triangle is the difference in elevation between the instrument's line of sight and the target.

The Elevation-Difference Equations

Surveyors express the line of sight either as a vertical angle (measured up or down from the horizontal, positive above) or as a zenith angle (measured from the vertical/straight up, so a horizontal sight is 90°). Total stations typically report zenith angles.

  • From a vertical angle and the horizontal distance: delta-elev = horizontal distance x tan(vertical angle).
  • From a vertical angle and the slope distance S: delta-elev = S x sin(vertical angle).
  • From a zenith angle z and the slope distance S: delta-elev = S x cos(z).

These are the same triangle written three ways. A positive result means the target line of sight is above the instrument's horizontal; a negative result means below.

From Line of Sight to Ground Elevation

The trig formula gives the rise or fall between the instrument's trunnion axis and the target, not between the ground points. To get the elevation of the ground at the target you must add the instrument height (HI) — the measured height of the instrument's center above its ground point — and subtract the target (or reflector) height — how high the prism sits above its ground point:

Elevtarget ground = Elevinstrument ground + HI + delta-elev - target height.

For example, if a total station occupies a point of elevation 500.00 ft with HI = 5.20 ft, measures a slope distance of 240.00 ft at a zenith angle of 84°00', and the prism height is 6.00 ft: delta-elev = 240.00 x cos(84°) = 240.00 x 0.10453 = +25.09 ft, so the target ground elevation = 500.00 + 5.20 + 25.09 - 6.00 = 524.29 ft.

content.text: Over long sights, trigonometric leveling needs a combined curvature-and-refraction correction (about +0.574 K^2 ft for a one-way sight, with K in miles - see the datums lesson). The most effective remedy is RECIPROCAL observation: measure the vertical angle in both directions and average, which largely cancels curvature, refraction, and instrument-index error.

content.alt: A total station occupying a point measures a slope distance and zenith angle to a prism on a higher point; the right triangle shows horizontal distance, slope distance, and the vertical elevation difference, with instrument height and target height labeled.
content.caption: Figure: Trigonometric leveling geometry - the vertical leg (delta-elev) is found from the slope distance and the vertical or zenith angle, then adjusted by instrument and target heights.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Trigonometric leveling computes elevation difference from a distance and a vertical/zenith angle.
delta-elev = d x tan(vertical angle) = S x sin(vertical angle) = S x cos(zenith angle).
Add the instrument height and subtract the target height to convert line-of-sight rise to a ground elevation.
Use curvature-and-refraction corrections and reciprocal observations for long sights.

flashcards.definition: Determining a difference in elevation indirectly from a measured slope (or horizontal) distance and a measured vertical/zenith angle, using right-triangle trigonometry.
flashcards.hint_1: How a total station gets elevations.

flashcards.definition: A vertical angle measured downward from the vertical (straight up); a horizontal line of sight reads 90 degrees. delta-elev = S x cos(zenith).
An angle measured up (+) or down (-) from the horizontal plane. With horizontal distance d: delta-elev = d x tan(vertical angle); with slope distance S: delta-elev = S x sin(vertical angle).
Measuring the vertical angle in both directions between two points and averaging, which largely cancels curvature, refraction, and instrument index error over long sights.

lessons.title: Level Instrument Adjustments
learning_objectives: Identify the principal lines and axes of a level and state the desired geometric relationships among them.
Define collimation (line-of-sight) error and explain its effect on rod readings.
Perform the two-peg test to detect and quantify collimation error, and compute the collimation correction.
Explain why reciprocal leveling and balanced sights protect a level run from residual instrument error.
key_takeaways: A level is in adjustment when the line of sight is parallel to the axis of the level (bubble/compensator), so it is horizontal when the instrument is level.
Collimation error is a line of sight that is not truly horizontal when the instrument is leveled; it grows with sight distance.
The two-peg test isolates collimation error by comparing readings from a balanced midpoint setup with readings from an unbalanced setup.
Balancing backsight and foresight distances cancels collimation error even if the instrument is slightly out of adjustment.

content.html:
The Lines and Axes of a Level

A level is built around several reference lines that must hold a specific geometric relationship for the instrument to give a true horizontal line of sight. The principal ones are the vertical axis (about which the instrument rotates), the line of sight (the optical axis of the telescope defined by the crosshairs), and the axis of the level — the line that is horizontal when the bubble is centered (in a tilting level) or the reference that the automatic compensator establishes (in an automatic level). The fundamental adjustment requirement is that the line of sight be horizontal when the instrument is leveled — equivalently, that the line of sight be parallel to the axis of the level.

When that relationship is satisfied the level is in adjustment. When it is not, the line of sight tilts slightly up or down even though the bubble is centered, and every rod reading is biased.

Collimation Error

Collimation error (line-of-sight error) is the small angular departure of the line of sight from true horizontal when the instrument has been leveled. Because the error is angular, its effect on a rod reading is proportional to the sight distance: a line of sight that droops by a tiny angle reads a little low on a near rod and much lower on a far rod. This is a systematic error — it always acts in the same sense for a given instrument — which makes it both dangerous (it accumulates) and, fortunately, controllable.

The key insight is that collimation error behaves exactly like curvature and refraction: it depends on distance. So the same field discipline — balancing backsight and foresight distances — causes the collimation error to be identical in the BS and the FS at each setup, where it cancels in the difference. A surveyor who balances sights can run accurate levels with an instrument that is slightly out of adjustment.

content.text: Adjustment reduces the error at its source; good field technique (balanced sights, reciprocal observations) cancels whatever error remains. Professionals rely on BOTH - they check adjustment periodically and balance sights every setup.

content.html:
The Two-Peg Test

The two-peg test detects and quantifies collimation error. Drive two pegs (A and B) a known distance apart — commonly about 200 ft. Step 1 (balanced): set the level exactly midway between A and B and read the rod on each. Because the sights are equal, any collimation error is identical in both readings and cancels, so the difference of these two readings is the true elevation difference between A and B. Step 2 (unbalanced): move the level close behind one peg (say a few feet behind A) and again read both rods. Now the sight to the far peg B is much longer than the sight to the near peg A, so collimation error inflates the far reading. Compare the elevation difference computed here against the true difference from Step 1; the discrepancy reveals the collimation error and lets you compute the correct reading the far rod should show, which is what you adjust the instrument to.

content.label: Two-peg test: true difference and corrected far reading

sections.title: Two-peg test, worked numbers
sections.content: Midpoint: reading on A = 5.04, on B = 5.42, so true delta-H = 5.04 - 5.42 = -0.38 ft (B is lower). Near A: reading on A = 4.80; the correct far reading should be b = a - delta-H = 4.80 - (-0.38)... taking signs from the near setup, b should be 4.80 + 0.38 = 5.18 if A is higher. If the actual far reading is 5.23, the line of sight is high by 0.05 ft over that extra distance - the collimation error to remove by adjusting the crosshair (or to account for as a correction per 100 ft).

sections.title: Other level adjustments
sections.content: Besides collimation, a tilting level's bubble (vial) adjustment keeps the bubble centered when the line of sight is horizontal; the crosshair adjustment keeps the horizontal hair truly horizontal. Automatic levels rely on a compensator whose proper function is verified by the two-peg test rather than a vial. Always confirm the compensator is working (lightly tap the instrument; the reading should return).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A level is in adjustment when the line of sight is horizontal (parallel to the level axis) once the instrument is leveled.
Collimation error tilts the line of sight; its effect on a rod reading grows with sight distance.
The two-peg test compares a balanced midpoint setup (true difference) with an unbalanced setup to quantify collimation error.
Balancing backsights and foresights cancels residual collimation error even when the instrument is slightly out of adjustment.

flashcards.definition: The angular departure of a level's line of sight from true horizontal when the instrument is leveled; its effect on a rod reading is proportional to the sight distance.

A field procedure that quantifies collimation error by comparing the elevation difference between two pegs measured from a balanced midpoint setup (true) with that measured from an unbalanced setup near one peg.
The optical axis of a level's telescope defined by the crosshairs; it must be horizontal when the instrument is leveled for the level to be in adjustment.
The gravity-actuated optical-mechanical device in an automatic level that automatically renders the line of sight horizontal; its function is verified with the two-peg test.

lessons.title: Benchmark Networks and Vertical Datums
learning_objectives: Define a benchmark and a vertical datum and explain the role of a benchmark network in vertical control.
Describe the modern North American vertical datum NAVD 88 and contrast it with the older NGVD 29.
Apply the combined curvature-and-refraction correction to long level or trig-leveling sights.
Compute the misclosure of a closed level loop and distribute the correction to balance (adjust) the loop.
key_takeaways: A benchmark is a permanent point of known elevation; a connected set forms the vertical-control network on which projects depend.
NAVD 88 is the current official vertical datum for the United States, replacing NGVD 29; elevations differ between them.
Curvature and refraction make a long horizontal sight read high; the combined correction is about 0.574 K^2 ft with K in miles.
A closed level loop should return to its start with zero misclosure; the actual misclosure is distributed to adjust the loop.

content.html:
Benchmarks and Vertical Control

A benchmark (BM) is a permanent, recoverable physical point whose elevation has been established by leveling and is held fixed for future work — a brass disk set in concrete or bedrock, a bolt, or a stable monument. A connected collection of benchmarks forms a vertical-control network: the framework of known elevations to which all project leveling is tied. Just as horizontal control gives every survey a common positional reference, vertical control gives every elevation a common datum so that grades, floodplains, and as-builts from different crews and different years agree.

In the United States the national vertical-control network is maintained by the National Geodetic Survey (NGS). Surveyors begin and end runs on published NGS or other reliable benchmarks; the published elevation, its datum, and its order/class of accuracy all matter and must be recorded.

Vertical Datums: NAVD 88 and NGVD 29

A vertical datum is the reference surface from which elevations are reckoned — conceptually a level surface near mean sea level (the geoid). Two datums dominate U.S. practice. The older National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929 (NGVD 29) was defined by holding mean sea level at 26 tide gauges. The current official datum is the North American Vertical Datum of 1988 (NAVD 88), which is fixed to a single primary tidal benchmark (Father Point/Rimouski, Quebec) and a continent-wide releveling adjustment. Elevations are not the same in the two datums — the difference varies geographically (commonly a few tenths of a foot to over a foot across the conterminous U.S.). Always state which datum an elevation is on; mixing NAVD 88 and NGVD 29 values silently is a serious blunder.

content.text: Texas practice: published Texas elevations and most current projects use NAVD 88, but legacy plats, FEMA panels, and older record data may be on NGVD 29. Identify and record the datum (and the geoid model for GNSS-derived heights) on every project; never assume two elevation sources share a datum.

content.html:
Curvature and Refraction Correction

Because the earth's level surface curves away from a straight horizontal line of sight, a distant rod reads too high by the curvature amount; atmospheric refraction bends the line of sight back down and partially offsets curvature. The two are combined into a single correction that grows with the square of the sight distance. A convenient form for U.S. units is approximately 0.574 K² feet, where K is the sight distance in miles (equivalently about 0.0206 K² ft for K in thousands of feet). For ordinary differential leveling the correction is made negligible simply by balancing backsight and foresight distances, but for long one-way trig-leveling sights it must be applied explicitly or removed by reciprocal observation.

Loop Misclosure and Adjustment

A closed level loop starts and ends on the same benchmark (or ties two benchmarks of known elevation). In a perfect loop the elevation you carry all the way around returns exactly to the starting value, so the misclosure — the difference between the computed and known elevation at the close — should be zero. In practice small random errors produce a nonzero misclosure. If it falls within the allowable tolerance for the order of work (a function of loop length), the loop is accepted and the misclosure is distributed back through the intermediate elevations to adjust (balance) the loop — commonly in proportion to the number of setups or the distance leveled to each point. The corrected elevations then sum consistently with the fixed benchmark(s).

content.alt: A closed level loop starting and returning to one benchmark through several turning points, with the small misclosure at the close distributed proportionally among the intermediate points.
content.caption: Figure: A closed level loop - misclosure at the return to the benchmark is distributed among the intermediate elevations to balance the loop.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A benchmark is a permanent point of known elevation; a network of them provides vertical control.
NAVD 88 is the current U.S. vertical datum (replacing NGVD 29); elevations differ between datums, so always record the datum.
Curvature and refraction make long sights read high; the combined correction is about 0.574 K^2 ft (K in miles).
A closed level loop's misclosure (computed minus known at the close) is distributed to adjust the loop when within tolerance.

flashcards.definition: A permanent, recoverable physical point of known, fixed elevation, used as a reference for leveling and held as part of the vertical-control network.
flashcards.hint_1: Brass disk in concrete or bedrock.

flashcards.definition: The North American Vertical Datum of 1988, the current official U.S. vertical datum, fixed to a primary tidal benchmark at Father Point/Rimouski; it replaced NGVD 29 and gives different elevations than NGVD 29.
A combined correction (about 0.574 K^2 ft with K in miles) accounting for the earth's curvature making a long sight read high and atmospheric refraction partially offsetting it.
flashcards.hint_1: Eliminated by balancing sights or reciprocal observation.

flashcards.definition: The difference between the computed elevation and the known elevation when a closed level loop returns to its starting (or a fixed) benchmark; ideally zero, and distributed to adjust the loop.

lessons.title: Leveling Field Exercises
learning_objectives: Reduce a complete set of differential-level field notes, computing HI and every elevation and applying the arithmetic check.
Compute the misclosure of a closed level loop and judge it against an allowable tolerance.
Apply trigonometric leveling to find a point's elevation from field measurements.
Set up, run, and check a short level circuit following correct field procedure.
key_takeaways: Always reduce notes with the HI method and confirm the arithmetic check before leaving the field.
Close every control loop and compare its misclosure to the allowable tolerance for the order of work.
Trig leveling and differential leveling can be combined on the same project; track instrument and target heights carefully.
Good field habits - plumb rod, balanced sights, firm turning points, and immediate checks - prevent most leveling errors.

content.html:
Putting Leveling into Practice

This lesson ties together the procedures from the module through worked field exercises. Real leveling work is judged not by a single reading but by whether a run closes — whether the elevations carried out into the field come back consistent with the control you started from. The professional habit to build is simple: reduce your notes and run your checks before you break down the instrument, so that if a run fails to close you can re-level it on the spot rather than returning another day.

Work each exercise below with disciplined note-keeping: record backsights and foresights to 0.01 ft, keep the rod plumb, choose firm turning points, and balance your sight distances. Then verify the arithmetic and assess the misclosure.

Exercise 1 - Reduce a Differential Level Run

Given BM-100 at elevation 250.00 ft, reduce the following run and find the elevation of BM-200.

Setup 1: BS on BM-100 = 6.41; FS on TP1 = 2.13. Setup 2: BS on TP1 = 5.87; FS on TP2 = 3.02. Setup 3: BS on TP2 = 4.55; FS on BM-200 = 7.96.

Solution. HI₁ = 250.00 + 6.41 = 256.41; TP1 = 256.41 - 2.13 = 254.28. HI₂ = 254.28 + 5.87 = 260.15; TP2 = 260.15 - 3.02 = 257.13. HI₃ = 257.13 + 4.55 = 261.68; BM-200 = 261.68 - 7.96 = 253.72 ft. Check: sum BS = 16.83, sum FS = 13.11; 16.83 - 13.11 = +3.72 = 253.72 - 250.00. The arithmetic checks.

Exercise 2 - Loop Misclosure

A closed loop begins and ends on BM-A (elevation 412.500 ft). After running around the loop through several turning points, the computed elevation back at BM-A is 412.546 ft. The loop is 1.6 miles long.

Solution. Misclosure = computed - known = 412.546 - 412.500 = +0.046 ft. A common allowable tolerance for ordinary leveling is on the order of 0.05 ft × sqrt(distance in miles) = 0.05 × sqrt(1.6) = 0.063 ft. Since 0.046 ft < 0.063 ft, the loop is acceptable; the +0.046 ft is distributed (in proportion to setups or distance) back through the intermediate elevations to balance the loop.

Exercise 3 - Trigonometric Leveling

A total station at a point of elevation 600.00 ft (instrument height 5.30 ft) sights a prism (height 5.00 ft) on a hill. It measures a horizontal distance of 320.00 ft and a vertical angle of +6°30' above horizontal. Find the ground elevation at the prism.

Solution. delta-elev = horizontal distance × tan(vertical angle) = 320.00 × tan(6.5°) = 320.00 × 0.11394 = +36.46 ft. Ground elevation = 600.00 + 5.30 + 36.46 - 5.00 = 636.76 ft. (Over this short sight the curvature-and-refraction correction, about 0.574 × (320/5280)² = 0.002 ft, is negligible.)

content.text: Field-check rule of thumb: never leave a control point until (1) the arithmetic check balances and (2) the loop misclosure is within tolerance for the order of work. A failed run found in the field costs an hour; one found in the office costs a day.

sections.title: Running a short level circuit, step by step
sections.content:
1. Start on a known benchmark; record its elevation and datum.
  2. Set the level roughly midway to the first turning point; verify it is level (compensator working).
  3. Backsight the benchmark; compute HI = elevation + BS.
  4. Foresight the turning point on a firm pin; compute elevation = HI - FS.
  5. Advance the instrument, backsight the same TP, and repeat to the destination.
  6. Close the run back on a benchmark; compute the misclosure and check it against tolerance; adjust if accepted.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Reduce level notes with the HI method and confirm the arithmetic check before breaking down the instrument.
Compute loop misclosure (computed minus known at the close) and compare it to the allowable tolerance before accepting a run.
Trig leveling gives ground elevation from distance, vertical angle, instrument height, and target height.
Disciplined field habits - plumb rod, balanced sights, firm turning points, and immediate checks - prevent most leveling errors.

flashcards.definition: The maximum acceptable loop misclosure for a given order of leveling, commonly expressed as a constant times the square root of the loop length (e.g., 0.05 ft x sqrt(miles) for ordinary work).
Verifying, before leaving the field, that the arithmetic check balances and the loop misclosure is within tolerance, so any failed run can be re-leveled immediately.
Adjusting (balancing) a closed level loop by spreading its misclosure back through the intermediate elevations in proportion to the number of setups or the distance leveled.

quiz_questions.question_text: A level surface is best described as a surface that is:
options: Perfectly flat and planar over any distance
Everywhere perpendicular to the direction of gravity (an equipotential surface)
Always parallel to the line of sight of the level
Tangent to the earth only at the equator
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Everywhere perpendicular to the direction of gravity (an equipotential surface)
quiz_questions.explanation: A level surface is equipotential and everywhere perpendicular to gravity, so it is curved (still water lies on one). A flat plane is a horizontal surface, which is only tangent to the level surface at one point.

quiz_questions.question_text: A benchmark has an elevation of 245.60 ft. A backsight of 4.85 ft is read on it. What is the height of instrument (HI)?
quiz_questions.explanation: HI = benchmark elevation + backsight = 245.60 + 4.85 = 250.45 ft. The backsight is added to a known elevation to obtain the line-of-sight elevation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A backsight is always taken on a point of known elevation, while a foresight is taken on a point whose elevation is being determined.
quiz_questions.explanation: By definition a backsight (plus sight) is read on a point of known elevation and added to it to get the HI; a foresight (minus sight) is read on a new point and subtracted from the HI to get its elevation.

quiz_questions.question_text: The HI at a setup is 308.20 ft. A foresight of 6.14 ft is read on a turning point. What is the elevation of the turning point?
quiz_questions.explanation: Elevation = HI - FS = 308.20 - 6.14 = 302.06 ft. Foresights are subtracted from the HI to obtain a point's elevation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do surveyors keep backsight and foresight distances equal at each setup?
options: To make the rod easier to read at a constant focus
So that curvature, refraction, and collimation errors are equal in both readings and cancel in the BS - FS difference
To reduce the number of turning points required
Because the instrument cannot focus on unequal distances
quiz_questions.correct_answer: So that curvature, refraction, and collimation errors are equal in both readings and cancel in the BS - FS difference
quiz_questions.explanation: These three errors are all proportional to sight distance. Equal BS and FS distances make the error identical in each reading, so it cancels when the difference is taken.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a set of level notes the sum of the backsights is 18.40 ft and the sum of the foresights is 15.10 ft. The starting elevation was 500.00 ft. What must the final elevation be for the arithmetic check to pass?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sum BS - sum FS = 18.40 - 15.10 = +3.30 ft, which must equal final - starting elevation, so final = 500.00 + 3.30 = 503.30 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: The arithmetic check (sum BS - sum FS = last elevation - first elevation) proves that the field measurements are accurate.
quiz_questions.explanation: The arithmetic check only verifies that the note reduction (the math) is correct. Field accuracy is judged by closing the loop and checking the misclosure against tolerance.

quiz_questions.question_text: In profile leveling, an intermediate foresight (IFS) differs from a turning-point foresight because the intermediate foresight:
options: Records a ground elevation but does not advance the level circuit
Must be read more precisely than the backsight
Can only be taken on a benchmark
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Records a ground elevation but does not advance the level circuit
quiz_questions.explanation: An intermediate foresight gives a ground shot (Elev = HI - IFS) without changing the HI or carrying the elevation forward. Only turning points advance the circuit.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many feet from the beginning of a route is station 14+25?
quiz_questions.explanation: A full station is 100 ft, so 14+25 means 14 x 100 + 25 = 1,425 ft from the beginning of the route.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of cross-section leveling?
options: To establish a network of permanent benchmarks
To capture ground elevations perpendicular to the centerline so earthwork (cut/fill) volumes can be computed
To check the collimation of the level
To measure horizontal angles along a route
quiz_questions.correct_answer: To capture ground elevations perpendicular to the centerline so earthwork (cut/fill) volumes can be computed
quiz_questions.explanation: Cross-sections give the transverse ground profile at each station; compared with the design template they yield end areas used (via average-end-area) to compute earthwork volumes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using a zenith angle z and a slope distance S, the difference in elevation between the instrument line of sight and the target is:
quiz_questions.explanation: The zenith angle is measured from the vertical, so the vertical component of the slope distance is S x cos(z). (With a vertical angle from horizontal it would be S x sin or d x tan.)

quiz_questions.question_text: A total station measures a horizontal distance of 150.00 ft and a vertical angle of +4°00' above horizontal. What is the difference in elevation (rise) of the line of sight, to the nearest 0.01 ft?
quiz_questions.explanation: delta-elev = horizontal distance x tan(vertical angle) = 150.00 x tan(4°) = 150.00 x 0.06993 = 10.49 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: To convert the line-of-sight elevation difference from trigonometric leveling into the target's ground elevation, you should:
options: Add the target height and subtract the instrument height
Add the instrument height (HI) and subtract the target (reflector) height
Subtract both the instrument and target heights
Ignore instrument and target heights for short sights
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Add the instrument height (HI) and subtract the target (reflector) height
quiz_questions.explanation: Ground elevation = instrument-ground elevation + HI + delta-elev - target height. The trig delta-elev is between the trunnion axis and the prism, so you add the instrument height and subtract the prism height.

quiz_questions.question_text: Collimation (line-of-sight) error in a level:
options: Has the same effect on every rod reading regardless of distance
Is random and averages out automatically
Produces a reading error proportional to the sight distance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Produces a reading error proportional to the sight distance
quiz_questions.explanation: Collimation error is an angular tilt of the line of sight, so its effect on a rod reading grows with distance. This is why balancing BS and FS distances cancels it.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the two-peg test, why is the elevation difference obtained from the midpoint (balanced) setup taken as the TRUE difference?
options: Because the instrument is closer to both pegs
Because equal sight distances make collimation error identical in both readings, so it cancels in the difference
Because refraction does not act at the midpoint
Because the rod is read only once
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because equal sight distances make collimation error identical in both readings, so it cancels in the difference
quiz_questions.explanation: At the midpoint the backsight and foresight distances are equal, so any collimation error appears equally in both readings and cancels when their difference is taken, giving the true elevation difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: Balancing backsight and foresight distances can compensate for an instrument that is slightly out of collimation adjustment.
quiz_questions.explanation: Because collimation error is proportional to distance, equal BS and FS distances make the error cancel in the difference, so accurate levels can be run even with a slightly maladjusted instrument.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about NAVD 88 and NGVD 29 is correct?
options: They are identical and the names are interchangeable
NAVD 88 is the current official U.S. vertical datum and gives different elevations than NGVD 29
Both are horizontal datums, not vertical datums
quiz_questions.correct_answer: NAVD 88 is the current official U.S. vertical datum and gives different elevations than NGVD 29
quiz_questions.explanation: NAVD 88 replaced NGVD 29 as the official vertical datum; elevations differ between them (varying geographically), so the datum must always be recorded.

quiz_questions.question_text: A closed level loop starts and ends on BM-7 (elevation 318.200 ft) but the computed elevation on returning is 318.235 ft. What is the misclosure?
quiz_questions.explanation: Misclosure = computed at close - known = 318.235 - 318.200 = +0.035 ft. If within tolerance it is distributed back through the intermediate elevations to balance the loop.

quiz_questions.question_text: Over a one-way sight of 0.50 mile, what is the approximate combined curvature-and-refraction correction using C = 0.574 K^2 ft (K in miles)?
quiz_questions.explanation: C = 0.574 x (0.50)^2 = 0.574 x 0.25 = 0.1435, about 0.14 ft. The correction grows with the square of the sight distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: BM-1 has elevation 100.00 ft. A backsight of 5.50 ft is read on it; a foresight of 3.20 ft is read on point P from the same setup. What is the elevation of P?
Before breaking down the instrument at the end of a control level run, a surveyor should at minimum confirm that:
options: The arithmetic check balances and the loop misclosure is within tolerance
The benchmark disk is newly painted
Only the final foresight was recorded
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The arithmetic check balances and the loop misclosure is within tolerance
quiz_questions.explanation: Checking the arithmetic and the misclosure in the field lets a failed run be re-leveled immediately, which is far cheaper than discovering the failure in the office.

problem_templates.name: Height of Instrument from a benchmark
problem_templates.category: Leveling and Vertical Control — Differential Leveling
problem_templates.question_template: A benchmark has a known elevation of {{bm:f2}} ft. From an instrument setup, a backsight of {{bs:f2}} ft is read on the benchmark. Compute the Height of Instrument (HI), in feet.

solution_steps_template.title: Add the backsight to the benchmark elevation

problem_templates.explanation_template: The backsight is added to the known benchmark elevation: HI = {{bm:f2}} + {{bs:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft. The HI is the elevation of the level's horizontal line of sight.

problem_templates.name: Elevation of a point from HI and foresight
problem_templates.category: Leveling and Vertical Control — Differential Leveling
problem_templates.question_template: The Height of Instrument at a setup is {{hi:f2}} ft. A foresight of {{fs:f2}} ft is read on a new point. Compute the elevation of that point, in feet.

solution_steps_template.title: Subtract the foresight from the HI

problem_templates.explanation_template: The foresight is subtracted from the HI to get the new point's elevation: Elev = {{hi:f2}} - {{fs:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Trigonometric leveling elevation difference
problem_templates.category: Leveling and Vertical Control — Trigonometric Leveling
problem_templates.question_template: A total station measures a horizontal distance of {{dist:f2}} ft to a target and a vertical angle of {{vang:f2}} degrees above the horizontal. Compute the difference in elevation (rise of the line of sight to the target), in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: delta-elev = horizontal distance x tan(vertical angle) = {{dist:f2}} x tan({{vang:f2}}°) = {{dist:f2}} x {{tanv:f4}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Level-loop misclosure from sum BS and sum FS
problem_templates.question_template: A closed level loop returns to its starting benchmark. The sum of all backsights is {{sumbs:f3}} ft and the sum of all foresights is {{sumfs:f3}} ft. For a perfectly closed loop these should be equal. Compute the loop misclosure (sum BS - sum FS), in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Around a closed loop the carried elevation should return to its start, so sum BS should equal sum FS. The misclosure is sum BS - sum FS = {{sumbs:f3}} - {{sumfs:f3}} = {{_answer}} ft, which (if within tolerance) is distributed to balance the loop.
